---
title: "Introduction to Git, GitHub, and GitHub Classroom"
author: "Jason Aubrey and Jeff Mei"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "Git, GitHub & GitHub Classroom"
    highlight-style: github-dark
    code-block-bg: true
    incremental: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Installing & Configuring Git {background-color="#0f3460"}

Check if you have it:
```bash
git --version
```

If not: [git-scm.com/downloads](https://git-scm.com/downloads)

**One-time setup (required):**
```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@email.com"
```

# Part 1: Why This Matters {background-color="#0f3460"}


## 🐦 A Job For The Birds {background-color="#16213e"}

**Data Scientist — Avian Observation Analytics**  
*eBird / Cornell Lab of Ornithology | Ithaca, NY*

**What you'd do:**

- Build species distribution models
- Analyze migration patterns & population trends
- Develop pipelines for large spatial datasets
- Contribute to open-source R and Python packages


**Salary:** $85,000–$105,000


---

## Would You Hire This Student? {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

Hesa Dodo (UArizona, Data Science and Ornithology '24) linked to this GitHub repo in his resume:

https://github.com/UArizona-Mathematics/for-the-birds



## Your GitHub Profile IS Your Portfolio {background-color="#533483"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### What employers look for

- Clear file and folder organization
- Readable, reasonably structured code
- **Informative README files**
- **Meaningful commit messages**
- Evidence of incremental work over time

> *They want to see how you work,  
> not just what you know.*
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### What makes a repo stand out

- README explains what the project does and how to run it
- Commits tell a story: `"Load data"` → `"Clean missing values"` → `"Add trend plot"`
- Code is organized, not cluttered with unused files
- Even small projects look professional when done with care

:::
::::


# Part 2: Git & GitHub {background-color="#0f3460"}


## The Problem Version Control Solves {background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out}
```
report_final.docx
report_final_v2.docx
report_final_v2_EDITED.docx
report_FINAL_reallyfinal.docx
report_FINAL_reallyfinal_JM_edits.docx
```
:::
::: {.fragment}
### Common pre-Git approaches:
- 📧 Emailing files back and forth
- 📁 Shared drives with conflicting versions
- 💾 Saving copies with different names

**These don't scale.** They can't answer:  
*Who changed what? When? Which version is current? How do we merge?*
:::
:::

---

## Git: The Tool {background-color="#0f3460"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Git runs on your computer.**

- Tracks changes to files over time
- Saves history as **commits** — labeled checkpoints
- Records *who* changed *what* and *when*
- Works **offline** — no internet needed

```bash
git init              # start tracking a folder
git add .             # stage your changes
git commit -m "..."   # save a checkpoint
```
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
### Key ideas

**Repository (repo)**  
A folder Git is tracking, including its full history.

**Commit**  
A saved snapshot with a message describing what changed.

**Clone**  
Copy an existing repo to your computer.
:::
::::


## GitHub: The Hub {background-color="#0f3460"}

**GitHub lives in the cloud.**

- Stores a copy of your git repository online
- Enables sharing and collaboration
- Widely used as a professional portfolio

```bash
git push    # upload commits to GitHub
git pull    # download others' changes
git clone   # copy a repo to your computer
```

## The Core Workflow {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
flowchart LR
    A[Edit files] --> B[git add .]
    B --> C["git commit -m '...'"]
    C --> D[git push]
    D --> E[GitHub ✅]
    F[git pull] --> A


    style A fill:#0f3460,color:#fff
    style B fill:#533483,color:#fff
    style C fill:#533483,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0f3460,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2d6a4f,color:#fff
    style F fill:#2d6a4f,color:#fff

```

## Commit Best Practices {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### ✅ Good commit messages
```
Clean and filter dataset
Add summary statistics table
Draft introduction section
Fix off-by-one error in loop
```
Commit when you finish a **logical step**.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### ❌ Poor commit messages
```
stuff
changes
worked on homework
asdfgh
final version
```
These tell no story and help no one — including future you.
:::
::::

> Commit history is part of the record of your work — and part of your portfolio.


# Part 3: GitHub Classroom {background-color="#0f3460"}

## What Is GitHub Classroom? {background-color="#16213e"}

A tool built on GitHub specifically for teaching and learning. GitHub Classroom is:

- A way to distribute assignments via git
- A system for collecting work via git
- A platform for viewing progress & giving feedback
- A way to run **automated tests** on student code

---

## Assignment Workflow {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

```{mermaid}
%% | echo: false
%% | fig-align: "center"
flowchart TD
    A["`🔗 Click assignment link`"] --> B["`📁 GitHub creates your private repo`"]
    B --> C["⬇️ Clone to your computer"]
    C --> D["📖 Read README.md"]
    D --> E["✏️ Edit files / do the work"]
    E --> F["git add . && git commit -m '...'"]
    F --> G["git push"]
    G --> H["✅ Submitted + autograded!"]
    F --> E

    style A fill:#533483,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2d6a4f,color:#fff
```

---

## Submission & Deadlines {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.callout-warning}
## There is NO "Submit" button.
:::

Your work is submitted based on:

1. **What has been committed**, AND
2. **What has been pushed to GitHub** by the deadline

Instructors view your repo directly — they see your **full commit history**, not just the final result.

**Checklist before every deadline:**
```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Final submission"
git push
# Then refresh GitHub in your browser and confirm your changes are there!
```

## How Autograding Works {background-color="#533483"}

When students push, GitHub Classroom automatically runs instructor defined tests against their code.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Behind the scenes:**

- Every push triggers instructor defined GitHub Actions
- It runs the code and checks output against expected results
- If all tests pass, you students get a ✅; if not, they get a ❌ and can fix and push again.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**What this means for students:**

- Should push early and often to check your progress
- A ❌ is not a catastrophe — fix and push again
- Tests run on your *committed and pushed* code, not unsaved edits
- **Final grade uses the last push before the deadline**

:::
::::

## Individual vs. Group Projects {background-color="#0f3460"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Individual
- Each student has own private repo
- Only the student + course staff can see it
- Clear individual record of their work
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Group
- One **shared** repo for the whole group
- Everyone clones the same repo
- Git records *who* contributed *what*
- Best practices require more git sophistication (e.g. branches, pull requests) but the core workflow is the same
```bash
git pull  # ← do this every time you sit down
```
:::
::::

# Part 4: Hands-On Demo {background-color="#2d6a4f"}

## 🐦 for-the-birds {background-color="#2d6a4f"}

### Accept the assignment now:

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;"}
[classroom.github.com/a/3Pd5JHnh](https://classroom.github.com/a/3Pd5JHnh)
:::

Starter repo: [github.com/UArizona-Mathematics/for-the-birds](https://github.com/UArizona-Mathematics/for-the-birds)

> While you're accepting, GitHub Classroom is creating a **private copy** of the starter repo just for you.

::: {.notes}
Have everyone click the link now. While they wait, explain what's happening: GitHub Classroom is forking the starter repo into the course organization and setting up access permissions automatically.
:::

---

## Demo Step 1: Explore Your Repo {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

Once your repo is created on GitHub:

1. Notice it says **Private** — only you and course staff can see it
2. Read the **`README.md`** file

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
## Always read the README first.
It tells you all about the code in the repo.  
It's also the first thing a potential employer reads.
:::

::: {.notes}
Show on screen. Navigate to the newly created repo, point out the Private badge, open README.md and read it aloud.
:::

---

## Demo Step 2: Clone to Your Computer {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

Copy the HTTPS URL from your repo on GitHub, then:

```bash
git clone <YOUR-REPO-URL>
cd for-the-birds-<your-username>
ls    # confirm the files are there (or dir on Windows)
```

Or in an IDE often something like: File → Clone Repository → paste your URL.

> Your local copy is now connected to GitHub.

::: {.notes}
Demo live in terminal. Show the output, then ls to show the files are there.
:::

---

## Demo Step 3: Fix the Typo {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

Open `birds.py` in your editor. There's a bug — find it and fix it.

```bash
# Run the code to see what happens:
python birds.py
```

Fix the typo, then run again to confirm it works.

::: {.callout-note}
Don't commit yet — we have more changes to make.
:::

::: {.notes}
Demo live. Show the error, find the typo, fix it, run again to confirm.
:::

---

## Demo Step 4: Add a Bird to the List {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

Following the pattern already in the code, add a new bird to the list.

```bash
# Run again to confirm your new bird appears:
python birds.py
```

> Pick a real bird. Extra credit if it's one you've actually seen. 🦅

::: {.notes}
Demo live. Add a bird, run the code, confirm output includes the new bird.
:::

---

## Demo Step 5: Edit the README {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

Open `README.md` and improve it.


::: {.notes}
Demo live. Write a short but real README. Contrast with the "done" example from earlier in the presentation.
:::

---

## Demo Step 6: Commit Your Work {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

```bash
git status         # see what's changed
git add .
git status         # confirm what's staged
git commit -m "Fix typo, add [your bird], update README"
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
Your changes are saved as a checkpoint — but still only on your computer.  
Nothing is on GitHub yet.
:::

---

## Demo Step 7: Push & Watch the Autograder {background-color="#2d6a4f"}

```bash
git push
```

Then on GitHub:

1. Go to your repo
2. Refresh the page to see your commit and message
4. ✅ green = passing · ❌ red = something to fix and push again

. . .

::: {.callout-important}
## Submitted!
Your work is now visible to instructors.  
If you get a ❌, fix the issue, commit again, and push again.
:::

::: {.notes}
Push live, then navigate to Actions tab and show the workflow running. This is the payoff — show the green checkmark appearing.
:::

---

## The Full Loop {background-color="#16213e"}

```bash
# One time to get started:
git clone <repo-url>

# Every work session:
git pull                       # get latest (essential for groups)

# As you work:
git add .
git commit -m "clear message"
git push                       # submits + triggers autograder
```

. . .

> **Follow this sequence consistently and most Git problems can be avoided.**

## Helpful Resources {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

| Resource | URL |
|----------|-----|
| GitHub Docs | [docs.github.com/en/get-started](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started) |
| GitHub Classroom Docs | [docs.github.com/en/classroom](https://docs.github.com/en/classroom) |
| Git Handbook | [guides.github.com/introduction/git-handbook](https://guides.github.com/introduction/git-handbook/) |
| Pro Git (free book) | [git-scm.com/book/en/v2](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2) |
